title: The Great Scientific Revolutions
subtitle: Seven moments when the world changed its mind
description: A masterclass-length narrative course tracing seven scientific revolutions — from Copernicus to information theory — and the people who drove them.
targetAudience: Curious readers who want the story of science, not a textbook
objectives: Understand what Kuhn meant by a paradigm shift
Recognise the social and political context of each revolution
Appreciate how scientific progress is actually messy
See why some ideas take centuries to win
Spot the recurring pattern across very different fields

modules.title: The Copernican Upheaval
modules.description: When the Earth stopped being at the centre.
objectives: Understand the Ptolemaic system's staying power

lessons.title: A Universe of Spheres
lessons.description: Fourteen hundred years of Ptolemy.
lessons.content: For fourteen centuries, Europe's model of the cosmos was Ptolemy's: a stationary Earth at the centre, surrounded by nested crystal spheres carrying the planets, the Sun, and the stars. It worked — it predicted eclipses well enough for everyday use.
keyPoints: Predictive success is not proof of a model's truth
Ptolemy's epicycles were a mathematical compromise that worked
The system had theological and philosophical inertia beyond science

lessons.title: The Man Who Waited
lessons.description: Why Copernicus sat on his manuscript for three decades.
lessons.content: Copernicus worked out the heliocentric model by 1514 but only published in 1543 — the year he died. He knew the backlash was coming, from theologians and from common sense. The Earth feels stationary.
keyPoints: Fear of consequences often delays publication, not certainty
Copernicus's preface, written by someone else, softened the claims
Intuition and observation can disagree for centuries

lessons.title: Tycho and Kepler
lessons.description: The observation that broke the old model.
lessons.content: Tycho Brahe made astronomical observations so precise that his data couldn't fit either Ptolemy or pure Copernicus. His assistant Kepler spent years forcing the data to speak — and eventually concluded the orbits were ellipses, not circles.
keyPoints: Better data often breaks older models before a new theory arrives
Kepler held Tycho's data for years and wrestled with it
Ellipses were more beautiful than epicycles, but only in retrospect

lessons.title: Galileo's Telescope
lessons.description: Seeing is finally believing.
lessons.content: When Galileo turned his telescope to Jupiter in 1610 and found moons orbiting it, he had the first visual proof that not everything orbited Earth. The Church's reaction is well-known. Science had crossed a line.
keyPoints: New instruments open new evidence categories
The Jupiter moons were the first definitive falsification
Institutional responses to new evidence are slower than evidence itself

modules.title: Newton Makes the Rules
modules.description: A single mathematics for heaven and earth.
objectives: Understand what was novel in Newton's synthesis
See how laws and mathematics became the template for science

lessons.title: The Plague Year
lessons.description: What a 23-year-old did at home in 1666.
lessons.content: When Cambridge closed because of plague, Newton went home for eighteen months. In that year he invented calculus, sketched the laws of motion, and began work on universal gravitation. He was twenty-three.
keyPoints: Concentrated isolation can produce extraordinary output
Newton sat on most of these ideas for twenty years before publishing
The apple story is largely apocryphal — but he did reason about gravity from the Moon

lessons.title: The Principia
lessons.description: One framework for everything that moves.
lessons.content: In 1687, Newton published the Principia Mathematica. It showed that the same gravitational force that made an apple fall also held the Moon in orbit. Terrestrial and celestial physics were unified for the first time.
keyPoints: Unification is a recurring driver of scientific revolutions
Newton wrote the Principia in Latin to signal seriousness
The mathematical style set a template followed for three centuries

lessons.title: What Got Left Unexplained
lessons.description: The cracks in the Newtonian edifice.
lessons.content: Newton's theory worked spectacularly — but the precession of Mercury's orbit wouldn't fit quite right. For two hundred years astronomers assumed there must be another planet tugging it. There wasn't. The crack would have to wait for Einstein.
keyPoints: Anomalies that don't go away are clues to the next revolution
Successful theories accumulate small failures patiently
The Mercury anomaly was measured, ignored, and then explained — eventually

modules.title: Darwin's Slow Bomb
modules.description: How a single idea reshaped biology.
objectives: Understand why Darwin delayed for twenty years
See what was revolutionary about natural selection

lessons.title: The Beagle Years
lessons.description: A young naturalist's five-year voyage.
lessons.content: Darwin boarded HMS Beagle in 1831 aged twenty-two. Over five years he filled notebooks with observations from South America and the Galápagos. He came home with boxes of specimens and questions he couldn't yet answer.
keyPoints: Fieldwork produces raw material that takes decades to interpret
The Galápagos finches came into focus only after Darwin had returned
Revolutionary ideas often start with careful cataloguing

lessons.title: Twenty Years of Delay
lessons.description: Why the Origin didn't appear until 1859.
lessons.content: Darwin worked out natural selection by 1844 but spent fifteen more years refining his case. The delay wasn't cowardice alone — he knew the evidence had to be overwhelming to survive the backlash he correctly anticipated.
keyPoints: Wallace's independent discovery finally forced Darwin's hand
Darwin and Wallace announced the theory jointly in 1858
A well-prepared case can survive hostile reception; a half-prepared one can't

lessons.title: The Mechanism
lessons.description: Variation, inheritance, selection — and that's it.
lessons.content: Natural selection requires only three ingredients: traits vary, traits are heritable, and some traits confer reproductive advantage. Given those three, evolution follows deductively. The power of the theory is in its minimalism.
keyPoints: Darwin didn't know the mechanism of inheritance — Mendel's work was still obscure
The three-ingredient structure is elegant but easy to misunderstand
Social Darwinism was a misappropriation, not an implication

quiz.question: Which was the primary reason Darwin delayed publishing On the Origin of Species?
options: Lack of supporting evidence in his lifetime
His understanding that the theory would be resisted and needed an airtight case
Legal disputes over the voyage of the Beagle
quiz.explanation: Darwin knew the theological and scientific establishment would resist the theory, so he spent fifteen years assembling as watertight a case as he could manage.

modules.title: The Germ Idea
objectives: Trace the overthrow of miasma theory
See how experimental proof looks in practice

lessons.title: Before Germs
lessons.description: The theory that bad air made you sick.
lessons.content: Into the 19th century, educated opinion held that disease came from bad air — miasma — rising from swamps and corpses. It wasn't silly: there was a correlation between foul places and sickness, so the model half-worked.
keyPoints: Wrong theories usually contain a kernel of useful correlation
Miasma theory motivated useful sanitation reforms even though the mechanism was wrong
Half-correct ideas can hold ground for decades

lessons.title: Semmelweis's Hands
lessons.description: The doctor who told obstetricians to wash their hands — and got fired.
lessons.content: In 1840s Vienna, Ignaz Semmelweis noticed that obstetricians who went straight from the morgue to the delivery room were killing mothers with childbed fever. Hand-washing dropped mortality dramatically. Colleagues rejected the finding and drove him from the hospital.
keyPoints: Correct findings can be rejected because of how they're delivered
Semmelweis's communication style hurt his cause badly
The tragedy is that he was right, and patients died during the delay

lessons.title: Pasteur and Koch
lessons.description: The definitive experiments.
lessons.content: Pasteur's swan-necked-flask experiment in 1859 killed the theory of spontaneous generation. Koch's postulates, two decades later, provided a rigorous procedure for identifying the specific microbe causing a specific disease. The game was over.
keyPoints: Well-designed experiments can decisively kill a competing theory
Koch's postulates formalised what scientific proof should look like
Microbiology became a real discipline almost overnight once the framework stuck

modules.title: Einstein's Year
modules.description: 1905, and four papers that broke physics.
objectives: Understand what Einstein changed in our view of space and time
See what a genuine paradigm shift looks like

lessons.title: The Patent Clerk
lessons.description: How a job reviewing patents gave Einstein time to think.
lessons.content: Einstein finished his day job at the Bern patent office by early afternoon, leaving the rest of the day to physics. In 1905 he published four papers, any one of which would have been a career. Together they redefined what space, time, mass, and light were.
keyPoints: Being outside mainstream academia can be an advantage
Mental space matters — creativity needs unrushed time
Revolutionary work can come from someone with no institutional power

lessons.title: Special Relativity
lessons.description: Space and time are not what we thought.
lessons.content: Einstein started from two postulates: the laws of physics are the same for all inertial observers, and the speed of light is constant. From those, he derived that time and length are relative to the observer — a conclusion that violates daily intuition.
keyPoints: Simultaneity is not absolute — it depends on the observer
Mass and energy are interchangeable (E = mc²)
The derivation is mathematically short but philosophically seismic

lessons.title: General Relativity
lessons.description: Gravity as curved spacetime.
lessons.content: By 1915, Einstein had generalised the theory to include gravity. Matter curves spacetime; objects move along geodesics in that curved geometry. It took experiments during a 1919 eclipse to confirm it — and then the world knew his name.
keyPoints: The 1919 Eddington eclipse expedition made Einstein world-famous overnight
General relativity subsumes Newtonian gravity as a low-velocity limit
The geometric view of gravity was a genuine paradigm shift

lessons.title: Mercury Again
lessons.description: The anomaly Newton couldn't explain.
lessons.content: Remember Mercury's odd orbital precession? General relativity predicts it exactly. Einstein said that moment of discovery gave him heart palpitations. A 250-year anomaly had finally found its explanation.
keyPoints: Anomalies can wait centuries for resolution
A new theory's first triumph is often resolving an old anomaly
Einstein confirmed Mercury before publishing — he didn't want to be wrong

modules.title: The Quantum Strangeness
objectives: Understand what makes quantum mechanics philosophically difficult
See why the founders never fully agreed about it

lessons.title: The Problem with Light
lessons.description: Waves or particles? Yes.
lessons.content: At the turn of the 20th century, light seemed to be a wave. But experiments with the photoelectric effect showed it behaving like particles. Einstein's 1905 paper on this (one of the four) was the one that won him the Nobel.
keyPoints: The Nobel wasn't for relativity, which was still too controversial
Wave-particle duality was the first sign that classical concepts wouldn't cut it
Quantum theory was built from experimental anomalies, not theory first

lessons.title: The Copenhagen Interpretation
lessons.description: Observation creates reality — maybe.
lessons.content: Bohr, Heisenberg, and Born developed an interpretation in which particles don't have definite properties until measured. The math works perfectly; the philosophy has never stopped being debated.
keyPoints: The Copenhagen view was pragmatic — shut up and calculate
Einstein famously hated it: 'God does not play dice.'
Ninety years on, the debate continues

lessons.title: EPR and Bell
lessons.description: Einstein's protest and its experimental resolution.
lessons.content: Einstein, Podolsky, and Rosen argued in 1935 that quantum mechanics must be incomplete because it implied 'spooky action at a distance.' In the 1960s, John Bell showed how to test it experimentally. By 2020s experiments, Einstein had lost — entanglement is real.
keyPoints: Philosophy can become physics once someone designs the right experiment
Bell's theorem rules out local hidden-variable theories
The 2022 Nobel Prize rewarded the experimentalists who confirmed entanglement

lessons.title: A Theory That Works Too Well
lessons.description: Why quantum is the most accurate theory we have.
lessons.content: Quantum electrodynamics predicts the electron's magnetic moment to twelve decimal places — matching experiment exactly. No theory in human history has been tested more precisely. And yet we still can't agree what it means.
keyPoints: Predictive success doesn't guarantee interpretive clarity
Quantum's accuracy is the most extreme in the history of science
The field split — pragmatists calculate, philosophers argue

modules.title: Information as Substance
modules.description: The revolution hiding in plain sight.
objectives: See why it connects fields that shouldn't connect

lessons.title: Shannon at Bell Labs
lessons.description: A 1948 paper that started a discipline.
lessons.content: In 1948, Claude Shannon published 'A Mathematical Theory of Communication.' It defined information as the reduction of uncertainty, measured in bits. Telecom engineers got a tool; everyone else eventually realised it applied to their problems too.
keyPoints: Shannon wasn't trying to start a revolution — he was solving an engineering problem
The formal definition of a bit dates from this paper
Information theory later unified problems in genetics, neuroscience, and finance

lessons.title: Information Meets Physics
lessons.description: Entropy, from thermodynamics to bits.
lessons.content: Shannon's entropy looked suspiciously like the entropy physicists had been using for a century. The connection turned out to be real — at a deep level, information and thermodynamic entropy are the same quantity.
keyPoints: Two fields independently discovered the same mathematics
Landauer's principle connects information to actual energy dissipation
Black-hole entropy is a frontier where information becomes physics

lessons.title: The Modern World
lessons.description: Why every layer of the information age traces back to 1948.
lessons.content: Error-correcting codes, compression, cryptography, and digital storage all trace their mathematical roots to Shannon. The smartphone in your pocket is a stack of applied information theory a metre deep.
keyPoints: Theoretical work often pays off on a multi-decade timescale
Information theory is the infrastructure of the digital age
Shannon's quiet revolution may be the biggest of the seven

quiz.question: In Shannon's information theory, what does a 'bit' represent?
options: A physical storage location in memory
A unit for measuring the reduction of uncertainty
A binary string of any length
An abbreviation for 'binary digit' and nothing more
quiz.explanation: Shannon defined the bit as a measure of information content — specifically, the amount of uncertainty resolved by a single yes/no answer.
